epigraph.text: And the house that King Solomon built for the Lord was sixty cubits long, twenty cubits wide, and thirty cubits high.

content.text:
Having explored the properties of two-dimensional figures, we now turn 
our attention to three-dimensional space. The world we inhabit is not 
flat—buildings rise toward the sky, containers hold water, and the 
earth itself is a sphere suspended in the heavens. Understanding how 
to measure the surface area and volume of solid figures is essential 
for architecture, engineering, and appreciating the created order.

In this section, we study two fundamental types of solids: prisms and 
cylinders. Both share a key structural feature—two congruent, parallel 
bases connected by lateral faces. This common structure leads to elegant, 
unified formulas for their surface area and volume.

content.title: Prism
content.content:
A **prism** is a polyhedron with two congruent, 
parallel polygonal faces called **bases**. 
The remaining faces, called **lateral faces**, 
are parallelograms formed by connecting corresponding vertices of 
the bases.

content.text: Prisms are named according to the shape of their bases:

content.title: Right Prism and Oblique Prism
content.content:
A prism is called a **right prism** if the 
lateral edges are perpendicular to the bases. In a right prism, all 
lateral faces are rectangles.

A prism is called an **oblique prism** if 
the lateral edges are not perpendicular to the bases. In an oblique 
prism, the lateral faces are parallelograms (not necessarily rectangles).

content.text:
The **height** of a prism is the perpendicular 
distance between the two bases. For a right prism, the height equals 
the length of any lateral edge.
Lateral Area and Surface Area of Prisms

content.title: Lateral Area and Surface Area
content.content:
The **lateral area** of a prism is the sum 
of the areas of all lateral faces (not including the bases).

The **surface area** (or **total 
surface area**) of a prism is the sum of the lateral area and the areas 
of both bases.

content.text:
Consider a right prism with a polygonal base. If we "unroll" the 
lateral surface, we obtain a rectangle whose width equals the height 
of the prism and whose length equals the perimeter of the base.

content.title: Lateral and Surface Area of a Right Prism
content.content:
Let a right prism have base perimeter $P$, base area $B$, and height $h$. Then:

- **Lateral Area:** $L = Ph$

- **Surface Area:** $S = L + 2B = Ph + 2B$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For a right prism, each lateral face is a rectangle. If the base is 
an $n$-sided polygon with sides of lengths $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_n$, 
then the lateral faces are rectangles with dimensions $s_1 \times h$, 
$s_2 \times h$, …, $s_n \times h$.

The lateral area is:
\[
L = s_1 h + s_2 h + \cdots + s_n h = h(s_1 + s_2 + \cdots + s_n) = Ph
\]

Adding the two bases:
\[
S = L + 2B = Ph + 2B \qedhere
\]

content.title: Surface area of a triangular prism
content.solution:
First, we find the hypotenuse of the triangular base using the 
Pythagorean Theorem:
\[
c = \sqrt{6^2 + 8^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ cm}
\]

The perimeter of the base is:
\[
P = 6 + 8 + 10 = 24 \text{ cm}
\]

The area of the base is:
\[
B = \frac{1}{2}(6)(8) = 24 \text{ cm}^2
\]

Using the surface area formula:
$$
S &= Ph + 2B \\
 &= (24)(12) + 2(24) \\
 &= 288 + 48 \\
 &= 336 \text{ cm}^2
$$

content.title: Surface area of a rectangular prism
content.problem:
A rectangular prism (box) has length 10 inches, width 6 inches, and 
height 4 inches. Find its surface area.
content.solution:
For a rectangular prism, we can use the general formula or compute 
the area of each face directly. The box has three pairs of congruent 
rectangular faces:

- Top and bottom: $10 \times 6 = 60$ square inches each

- Front and back: $10 \times 4 = 40$ square inches each

- Left and right: $6 \times 4 = 24$ square inches each

Surface area:
\[
S = 2(60) + 2(40) + 2(24) = 120 + 80 + 48 = 248 \text{ square inches}
\]

Alternatively, using $S = Ph + 2B$ with base $10 \times 6$:
$$
P &= 2(10) + 2(6) = 32 \text{ inches} \\
B &= 10 \times 6 = 60 \text{ square inches} \\
S &= (32)(4) + 2(60) = 128 + 120 = 248 \text{ square inches}
$$

content.text:
**Volume** measures the amount of space a solid 
occupies. We measure volume in *cubic units*—cubic centimeters, 
cubic inches, cubic feet, and so on.

The volume of a rectangular prism with length $\ell$, width $w$, 
and height $h$ is simply $V = \ell w h$. Since $\ell w$ equals the 
area of the base, this can be written as $V = Bh$. Remarkably, this 
same formula works for *all* prisms, regardless of the shape 
of the base.

content.title: Volume of a Prism
content.content:
The volume of a prism with base area $B$ and height $h$ is:
\[
V = Bh
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
This theorem follows from **Cavalieri's Principle**, 
named after the Italian mathematician Bonaventura Cavalieri (1598–1647):

content.title: Cavalieri's Principle
content.content:
If two solids have the same height and the same cross-sectional area 
at every level, then they have the same volume.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Volume of a triangular prism
content.problem:
Find the volume of the triangular prism from Example [ex:triangular-prism-sa] 
(base is a right triangle with legs 6 cm and 8 cm, height 12 cm).
content.solution:
We already found the base area: $B = \frac{1}{2}(6)(8) = 24$ cm$^2$.

The volume is:
\[
V = Bh = (24)(12) = 288 \text{ cm}^3
\]

content.title: Volume of a hexagonal prism
content.problem:
A regular hexagonal prism has a base edge of 4 cm and a height of 10 cm. 
Find its volume.
content.solution:
A regular hexagon with side length $s$ has area:
\[
B = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}s^2
\]

With $s = 4$:
\[
B = \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{2}(16) = 24\sqrt{3} \text{ cm}^2
\]

The volume is:
\[
V = Bh = (24\sqrt{3})(10) = 240\sqrt{3} \text{ cm}^3 \approx 415.7 \text{ cm}^3
\]

content.text:
A cylinder can be thought of as a prism with circular bases—a 
"circular prism." Just as a polygon with infinitely many sides 
approaches a circle, a prism with a regular $n$-gon base approaches 
a cylinder as $n$ increases.

content.title: Cylinder
content.content:
A **cylinder** is a solid with two congruent, 
parallel circular bases connected by a curved lateral surface. The 
**axis** of a cylinder is the segment 
connecting the centers of the two bases.

content.title: Right Cylinder and Oblique Cylinder
content.content:
A cylinder is a **right cylinder** if 
its axis is perpendicular to the bases. Otherwise, it is an 
**oblique cylinder**.

content.text:
If we "unroll" the lateral surface of a right cylinder, we obtain 
a rectangle. The height of this rectangle is the height $h$ of the 
cylinder, and the width is the circumference of the base, $2\pi r$.

content.title: Surface Area of a Right Cylinder
content.content:
The surface area of a right cylinder with radius $r$ and height $h$ is:
\[
S = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi rh = 2\pi r(r + h)
\]
where $2\pi r^2$ is the combined area of the two circular bases and 
$2\pi rh$ is the lateral area.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Surface area of a cylinder
content.problem: Find the surface area of a cylinder with radius 5 cm and height 12 cm.

content.title: Finding radius from surface area
content.problem:
A cylindrical can has height 8 inches and surface area $130\pi$ 
square inches. Find the radius of the can.
content.solution:
Let $r$ be the radius. Using $S = 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi rh$:
$$
130\pi &= 2\pi r^2 + 2\pi r(8) \\
130\pi &= 2\pi r^2 + 16\pi r \\
130 &= 2r^2 + 16r \quad \text{(divide by } \pi \text{)} \\
65 &= r^2 + 8r \quad \text{(divide by 2)} \\
r^2 + 8r - 65 &= 0 \\
(r + 13)(r - 5) &= 0
$$

Since radius must be positive, $r = 5$ inches.

content.text:
Since a cylinder is analogous to a prism with a circular base, its 
volume formula follows the same pattern: $V = Bh$, where $B = \pi r^2$ 
is the area of the circular base.

content.title: Volume of a Cylinder
content.content:
The volume of a cylinder with radius $r$ and height $h$ is:
\[
V = \pi r^2 h
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
By Cavalieri's Principle, this formula applies to both right and 
oblique cylinders—only the height matters, not whether the cylinder 
is tilted.

content.title: Volume of a cylinder
content.problem:
A water tank is a cylinder with radius 4 feet and height 10 feet. 
Find its volume in cubic feet. Then convert to gallons (1 cubic 
foot $\approx$ 7.48 gallons).
content.solution:
The volume is:
\[
V = \pi(4)^2(10) = \pi(16)(10) = 160\pi \text{ ft}^3 \approx 502.7 \text{ ft}^3
\]

Converting to gallons:
\[
502.7 \times 7.48 \approx 3760 \text{ gallons}
\]

content.title: Comparing volumes
content.problem:
Two cylinders have the same volume. Cylinder A has radius 3 and 
height 16. Cylinder B has radius 4. Find the height of Cylinder B.
content.solution:
Let $h$ be the height of Cylinder B.

Volume of A: $V_A = \pi(3)^2(16) = 144\pi$

Volume of B: $V_B = \pi(4)^2(h) = 16\pi h$

Since $V_A = V_B$:
$$
144\pi &= 16\pi h \\
h &= \frac{144\pi}{16\pi} = 9
$$

The height of Cylinder B is 9.

content.title: Rate of filling
content.problem:
A cylindrical pool has diameter 24 feet and depth 5 feet. Water 
flows in at a rate of 30 cubic feet per minute. How long will it 
take to fill the pool?
content.solution:
The radius is $r = 12$ feet. The volume of the pool is:
\[
V = \pi(12)^2(5) = 720\pi \text{ ft}^3
\]

Time to fill at 30 ft$^3$/min:
\[
t = \frac{720\pi}{30} = 24\pi \text{ minutes} \approx 75.4 \text{ minutes}
\]

This is about 1 hour and 15 minutes.

marginNotes.text:
You shall make the altar of acacia wood, 
five cubits long and five cubits broad. The altar shall be square, 
and its height shall be three cubits.

Then he made the sea of cast metal. 
It was round, ten cubits from brim to brim, and five cubits high.

This is how you are to make it: the 
length of the ark 300 cubits, its breadth 50 cubits, and its height 
30 cubits.

And the vessel he was making of clay 
was spoiled in the potter's hand, and he reworked it into another 
vessel, as it seemed good to the potter to do.
